config.title: Berlin Accessibility - FAQ
config.subtitle: Quick answers for visitors planning step-free Berlin transport, sights and walking routes.

items.q: Is Berlin wheelchair accessible for tourists?
items.a: Berlin is partly accessible and improving, but it is not a city where you should improvise every route. Use official barrier-free route filters, check elevator status before travel, and choose plans with fewer transfers when step-free access is essential.

items.q: How do I plan step-free public transport in Berlin?
items.a: Use the BVG or VBB route planner with barrier-free settings, then check current elevator and escalator outages for U-Bahn and S-Bahn stations. If an elevator is essential, keep a bus, tram or taxi fallback.

items.q: Are Berlin buses and trams accessible?
items.a: Buses and modern trams are often useful step-free backups, especially when a station elevator is out. Still check the exact stop, crowding, ramp use and walking distance at both ends of the trip.

items.q: Which Berlin sights are better for accessible planning?
items.a: The Reichstag dome is a strong option with advance registration and accessible entry. Museum Island also publishes accessible services, while the TV Tower is not barrier-free for visitors with severe mobility issues.

items.q: Is the BerlinWalk route accessible for wheelchairs or strollers?
items.a: The core route is mostly flat and paved, with some cobblestones, bridges and normal city obstacles. If accessibility is a key concern, contact before booking so the day can be planned honestly.

items.q: What is the safest accessibility rule for a first Berlin day?
items.a: Choose one main area, avoid unnecessary transfers, check lifts shortly before departure, and keep a realistic backup. A simpler day usually beats a perfect route that depends on one elevator.
